Stack Overflow Questions Tags

Hot Network Questions

  • What is the difference between mut and &mut?
  • When should I use Arc vs Rc?
  • How does lifetimes work in Rust?

Why is Rust's borrow checker so strict about mutable references?

I keep running into compile errors where I have a mutable reference and a shared reference to the same value at the same time. Coming from C++ this feels overly restrictive. Is there a technical reason for this rule, or is it just a design choice?

For example, this fails to compile:

let mut v = vec![1, 2, 3];
let first = &v[0];
v.push(4); // error: cannot borrow `v` as mutable
println!("{}", first);
asked by user123 — 42 votes
127

The rule exists to guarantee memory safety without a garbage collector. If v.push(4) triggers a reallocation, the internal buffer moves to a new memory address — and first would point to freed memory. This is a use-after-free bug in C++.

Rust's borrow checker enforces the rule at compile time that a mutable reference (&mut T) and any shared reference (&T) to the same data cannot coexist. This eliminates an entire class of memory safety bugs.

The solution is to clone what you need before mutating, or restructure the code to separate the read and write phases.

let mut v = vec![1, 2, 3];
let first = v[0]; // copy the i32, don't borrow
v.push(4);
println!("{}", first); // safe
answered by ferris — 127 votes
45

To add to the accepted answer: this rule is called "aliasing XOR mutability" and it's a fundamental invariant of Rust's ownership model. It is not just a design quirk — it's the formal property that makes Rust's type system sound.

In practice you work around it by using Cell, RefCell, Mutex, or by restructuring your data access patterns to avoid the overlap entirely.

answered by lifetime_fan — 45 votes
